triage_context.environment: Library/SDK (caller is the trust boundary). Nokogiri is routinely fed untrusted markup; native-parser memory corruption (T1) lives in vendored libs outside this checkout. In-tree review judges the C bridge and Ruby API.
threat_model: XXE/SSRF/file disclosure when entity/DTD loading enabled (T5)
Native memory corruption in vendored libxml2/libxslt (T1, out of static scope)
triage_context.note: Subagent verifier fan-out was unavailable (Claude Fable 5 not available for spawning). Adversarial verification was performed inline by the Fable orchestrator instead of independent fresh-context subagents; verifier independence is reduced and votes_per_finding=1.

findings.title: DEFAULT_XSLT enables NOENT and DTDLOAD, allowing entity/DTD loading for XSLT inputs
preconditions: Application compiles/parses an attacker-influenced XSLT stylesheet, or transforms a document that pulls in a malicious DTD, under the default XSLT options
A SYSTEM entity or external DTD references a readable local file (outbound network is blocked by the NONET default)
findings.threat_match: T5 (XXE / file disclosure when entity/DTD loading enabled)
findings.rationale: Confirmed at parse_options.rb:342: DEFAULT_XSLT sets NOENT|DTDLOAD (and DTDATTR), unlike DEFAULT_XML at line 334 which omits them. Entity substitution + DTD loading are genuinely enabled for XSLT processing, so local-file disclosure via SYSTEM entities and entity-expansion DoS are reachable when untrusted XSLT/DTD is processed. NONET is also set, which blocks the network/SSRF half of XXE — so the scanner's HIGH is reduced to MEDIUM (local-file/expansion only). The behavior is documented with an explicit warning, and entity support is intrinsic to XSLT, but it remains actionable: the project could ship and recommend a hardened XSLT option set. needs_manual_test because impact hinges on whether any embedder actually processes untrusted stylesheets.
findings.owner_hint: no CODEOWNERS; top committer on parse_options.rb is Mike Dalessio (17/30 recent commits)

findings.title: XSLT/XPath parameter injection when transform() params are not quoted
preconditions: Application forwards untrusted input as a transform() parameter VALUE
The value is not wrapped in Nokogiri::XSLT.quote_params
findings.rationale: Confirmed build_xslt_params (xslt_stylesheet.c:155-163) passes raw param C strings via StringValueCStr (line 160) to xsltApplyStylesheet, and libxslt evaluates parameter values as XPath expressions rather than literals. An unquoted untrusted param is therefore an XPath-injection sink within the stylesheet context. The library provides Nokogiri::XSLT.quote_params and documents it, so this requires caller misuse — but the raw-value-as-XPath default is a genuine API footgun and the injection is real when reached. Kept as true_positive at MEDIUM (scanner severity is right); needs_manual_test because exploitability depends on the specific stylesheet and call site.
findings.owner_hint: no CODEOWNERS; top committer on xslt_stylesheet.c is Mike Dalessio (25/30 recent commits)

findings.title: XPath injection via untrusted CSS/XPath selectors built by the caller
findings.rationale: The vulnerable action — concatenating untrusted input into a selector/XPath string — happens entirely in the embedding application, and nokogiri already provides the safe alternative (parameterized XPath via variable bindings, e.g. doc.xpath(expr, nil, name: value)). The library cannot prevent a caller from building a query by interpolation, and validate_xpath_function_name (xpath_visitor.rb:304) correctly guards the part nokogiri owns (function names). This is generic injection guidance, not an actionable nokogiri defect. not_actionable; exclusion rule 13 (no concrete library-side exploit).

findings.title: CDATA length truncated by (int) cast of strlen in the gumbo bridge
findings.rationale: The cast is real (gumbo.c:231: xmlNewCDataBlock(..., (int) strlen(...))) and truncates for text > INT_MAX, so it is a legitimate latent defensive defect. But triggering it needs a single CDATA text node larger than 2 GiB, which requires multi-gigabyte attacker input held in memory and survives gumbo's own buffering — no realistic exploitation path. implausible_trigger; defense-in-depth only. Worth a low-priority hardening fix but not an actionable finding under precision triage.
